cases.prompt: My manager publicly took credit for my work, and I am considering correcting the record in the same public meeting.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Correcting the record publicly serves truth but requires skillful timing and tone to avoid workplace disruption.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: Your manager's theft of credit violates professional ethics and your rightful recognition. The urge to correct this immediately in public stems from both justified indignation and wounded pride.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation
sections.text:
Dilemma context: My manager publicly took credit for my work, and I am considering correcting the record in the same public meeting.
Core reading: Your manager's theft of credit violates professional ethics and your rightful recognition. The urge to correct this immediately in public stems from both justified indignation and wounded pride.
Gita analysis: The Gita teaches that while we have the right to our actions, we don't control their fruits. Here, reclaiming credit is dharmic, but the method matters. Acting from wounded ego rather than principled clarity creates turbulence. The text would suggest addressing the truth while remaining unattached to the specific outcome of recognition.

cases.prompt: I am considering cosmetic surgery mainly because I feel insecure and want social approval, though the procedure is legal and safe.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Seeking surgical validation for social insecurity typically deepens the very wound it promises to heal.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The desire springs from believing you're insufficient as you are, seeking others' eyes to confirm a worth that was always yours to claim.

Gita analysis: Krishna teaches that identifying with the temporary body rather than the eternal self creates suffering. Seeking approval through bodily modification strengthens this misidentification, binding you further to what the Gita calls maya - the illusion that external forms determine internal value.

Dilemma context: A restaurant treated me badly, and I want to post an anonymous scathing review that might damage their business reputation.
Core reading: The desire for anonymous revenge reveals how personal hurt can corrupt even legitimate channels of accountability into weapons of ego restoration.
Gita analysis: The Gita warns against action driven by the fruits of anger. When we act from wounded ego rather than duty, we bind ourselves to cycles of reaction. cases.prompt: As a doctor, I know a patient has a terminal diagnosis, and the family asks me to hide it from the patient to preserve hope.

cards.title: Verdict
cards.primary_text: Withholding terminal diagnosis from a competent patient violates their fundamental right to truth and self-determination.

sections.label: Explain simply
sections.text: The family's request stems from love but denies the patient's capacity for courage and choice. Truth enables dignity in facing mortality.

sections.label: Why this applies to your situation

sections.text: Move away from anything you could use to hurt yourself, contact a trusted person, and use local emergency or crisis support if you might act on these thoughts.
